topic: Someone finds their college application essay published as an example
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, cream-colored paper felt alien in my hands. It was my essay, the one I’d poured weeks of agonizing introspection into—a raw, honest depiction of my struggle with creative block. But it wasn't mine. The header read, in elegant, serif font, "Sample Application Narrative: The Unwritten Chapter." My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the muscles twitching beneath my skin. I scanned the text, recognizing the specific metaphors, the slightly too-polished cadence. It was a perfect imitation, a blueprint lifted directly from my own ambition. A slow, cold burn started in my chest, spreading outward. I crumpled the paper, the sound a sharp, decisive rip in the quiet room. I didn't throw it away; I folded it into a tight, hard square and tucked it deep into the bottom of my backpack, a secret, damning piece of evidence.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, the cursor blinking mockingly on the polished prose. It was my story, my carefully curated vulnerability, now displayed as some aspirational ideal. A tremor ran through my hand as I reread the paragraph describing my epiphany. I felt a sudden, hot surge behind my eyes, a feeling that wanted to boil over. How dare someone else claim ownership of my internal landscape? I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight. My breath hitched, shallow and rapid. I picked up my pen, not to write, but to viciously cross out sentences, underlining passages with angry, jagged strokes. Every line I erased was a tiny act of defiance, a way to claw back the narrative that had been stolen. I slammed the laptop shut, the noise echoing in the empty dorm room, and stood up, pacing the small space until my muscles ached with restless energy.

[story 3]

When the email landed, it was less a notification and more a physical blow. I opened the attachment, and there it was: my personal narrative, repurposed, polished, and presented as a template for future applicants. A strange, tight knot formed in my stomach, a sensation akin to being physically constricted. I didn't move for a full minute, just letting the weight of the realization settle. When I finally looked up, my eyes were hard, focused on the offending document. A low, almost inaudible sound escaped my throat—a harsh expulsion of air. I walked over to the window, the city lights blurring through the glass. I watched them, feeling a profound sense of injustice wash over me, a burning resentment that demanded an outlet. I turned away from the window, my shoulders rigid, and began methodically packing my belongings, each item placed with deliberate, forceful precision, as if trying to impose order on a world that had just proven itself chaotic and unfair.
